coredata.dc:title: Toward highly reversible aqueous zinc-ion batteries: nanoscale-regulated zinc nucleation via graphene quantum dots functionalized with multiple functional groups
coredata.dc:creator: Han, Weiwei
Lee, Hankyu
Liu, Yuzhen
Kim, Youjoong
Chu, Huaqiang
Liu, Guicheng
Yang, Woochul
coredata.dc:description: Rechargeable aqueous Zn-ion batteries have a promising application potential and represent competitive candidates in the field of large-scale energy storage. However, Zn metal is prone to uncontrolled dendrite formation, hydrogen evolution, and corrosion, all of which limit the reversibility of the corresponding batteries. Herein, a novel kind of nanosized and functionalized graphene quantum dots (F-GQDs) is decorated on a Zn anode via in situ electrochemical induction. These quantum dots (∼5 nm) can regulate Zn plating/stripping at the nanoscale. Furthermore, the high electronegativity of polar functional groups (–OH, –COOH, –NH2, and -SCN) on the GQDs results in strong Zn2+ affinity and the F-GQDs endow the Zn anode with high hydrophilicity, low nucleation energy barrier, and an evenly distributed electrical field. As a result, the F-GQDs-decorated Zn anode achieves superior Zn plating/stripping for greater than 450 h at 10 mA cm−2 and 5 mAh cm−2, with a low voltage hysteresis of 81 mV. Moreover, when coupled with MnO2 cathodes, the F-GQDs-decorated Zn enables the fabrication of Zn||MnO2 full batteries with significantly enhanced rate capability and long-term cycling performance (capacity retention of 78.6 % at 1 A/g after 500 cycles).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Aqueous Zn-ion batteries
Functionalized graphene quantum dots
Electrochemical induction
Nucleation
Dendrite-free

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Physical and chemical characteristics of F-GQDs. (a) Schematic illustration of the growth processes of F-GQDs. (b) TEM image of F-GQDs. (c) Absorption spectra of F-GQDs with and without ZnSO4. (d) FTIR spectrum of F-GQDs. XPS survey spectrum (e) along with high-resolution XPS profiles of (f) N 1 s and (g) S 2p states in F-GQDs.

Electrochemical performances of symmetric Zn||Zn cells and Zn||Ti half-cells. Galvanostatic cycling performance of symmetric cells using the electrolytes with or without F-GQDs at (a) 0.5 mA cm−2 with a plating/stripping capacity of 0.25 mAh cm−2 and (b) 10 mA cm−2 with a plating/stripping capacity of 5 mAh cm−2. Galvanostatic voltage profiles of Zn||Ti half-cells without (c) and with (d) F-GQDs at 5 mA cm−2 and a plating/stripping capacity of 2.5 mAh cm−2. (e) CE of Zn plating/stripping at 5 mA cm−2 and a plating/stripping capacity of 2.5 mAh cm−2.

In situ optical microscopy images of Zn deposition without (a) and with (b) F-GQDs. SEM images of plated Zn in symmetric cells without (c, d) and with (e, f) F-GQDs in the electrolyte after five cycles. 3D laser microscopy images of cycled Zn without (g) and with (h) F-GQDs. (i) Raman spectra of cycled Zn with and without F-GQDs.

(a) Atomic model of synthesized graphene quantum dots. (b) Adsorption energies of functional groups and H2O obtained from DFT calculations. (c) Tafel curves of Zn anodes in the electrolyte with and without F-GQDs. (d) Contact angles of Zn anodes with and without F-GQDs. Numerical simulations of electric field distributions for Zn anodes without (e) and with (f) F-GQDs.

Electrochemical performance of Zn||MnO2 full-batteries with and without F-GQDs. (a) CV plots at a scan rate of 0.1 mV s−1. (b) GCD profiles at a current density of 0.2 A/g. (c) Rate capabilities. (d) EIS profile and corresponding fitted equivalent circuit model (inset). (e) Long-term cycling stability at 1 A/g.

head.ce:title: Toward highly reversible aqueous zinc-ion batteries: nanoscale-regulated zinc nucleation via graphene quantum dots functionalized with multiple functional groups

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Dongguk University, Seoul 04620, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Dongguk University, Seoul 04620, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechanical Engineering, Yonsei University, Seoul 03722, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Mechanical Engineering, Yonsei University, Seoul 03722, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Energy and Environment, Anhui University of Technology, Ma’anshan 243002, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Energy and Environment, Anhui University of Technology, Ma’anshan 243002, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In situ fabrication of functionalized graphene quantum dots (F-GQDs) with multiple functional groups on a Zn anode suppresses dendrite formation and mitigates side reactions. The F-GQDs-decorated Zn adopts a non-dendritic morphology during repeated Zn plating/stripping and exhibits a long cycle life of 450 h at ultrahigh current density (10 mA cm−2) and areal capacity (5 mAh cm−2).

Rechargeable aqueous Zn-ion batteries have a promising application potential and represent competitive candidates in the field of large-scale energy storage. However, Zn metal is prone to uncontrolled dendrite formation, hydrogen evolution, and corrosion, all of which limit the reversibility of the corresponding batteries. Herein, a novel kind of nanosized and functionalized graphene quantum dots (F-GQDs) is decorated on a Zn anode via in situ electrochemical induction. These quantum dots (∼5 nm) can regulate Zn plating/stripping at the nanoscale. Furthermore, the high electronegativity of polar functional groups (–OH, –COOH, –NH2, and -SCN) on the GQDs results in strong Zn2+ affinity and the F-GQDs endow the Zn anode with high hydrophilicity, low nucleation energy barrier, and an evenly distributed electrical field. As a result, the F-GQDs-decorated Zn anode achieves superior Zn plating/stripping for greater than 450 h at 10 mA cm−2 and 5 mAh cm−2, with a low voltage hysteresis of 81 mV. Moreover, when coupled with MnO2 cathodes, the F-GQDs-decorated Zn enables the fabrication of Zn||MnO2 full batteries with significantly enhanced rate capability and long-term cycling performance (capacity retention of 78.6 % at 1 A/g after 500 cycles).

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Rechargeable lithium-ion batteries dominate the market of secondary batteries for consumer electronics and electric vehicles [1–4]. Nevertheless, their long-term inherent safety hazards and the limited availability of lithium resources have prompted the exploration of other types of secondary batteries. Rechargeable aqueous batteries (e.g., Zn2+, Al3+, and NH4 +) are attracting considerable attention because of their intrinsic safety [5–7]. In addition, aqueous batteries have the benefits of low cost, high ionic conductivity, and assembly in air. Therefore, they are considered the most promising candidates for large-scale grid energy storage. Among aqueous batteries, Zn metal is an attractive anode material for aqueous Zn-ion batteries (ZIBs), owing to its high theoretical gravimetric capacity (820 mAh/g) and adequate redox potential (-0.76 V vs SHE) [8,9]. However, Zn anodes generally suffer from the formation of parasitic dendrites and the occurrence of side reactions (including zinc corrosion and hydrogen evolution), which impair their Coulombic efficiency (CE) and service lifespan [10–12].

Previous studies have focused on the development of techniques to control the plating/striping morphologies of Zn in aqueous electrolytes and on the production of dendrite-free ZIBs with long-term stability. The growth of Zn dendrites is often related to the uneven Zn plating/stripping, while low CE values and insufficient utilization originate from the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) and the formation of irreversible byproducts on the Zn anode. Under real conditions, these unfavorable aspects result in an unstable CE and impair the capacity decay of ZIBs [13]. Current studies of anodes of aqueous ZIBs mainly focus on the modification of Zn metal and the introduction of electrolyte additives [10,14,15]. Modifying the Zn metal can partially limit the dendrite formation and side reactions. Reported methods include surface coating, alloying, and optimization of the Zn structure, using materials such as artificial Sc2O3 [16], β-polyvinylidene fluoride (β-PVDF) [17], MXene [18], ZnSe [19–21], and 3D Zn foil [22]. Nonetheless, the incompatibility of the artificial coatings with the Zn substrate may result in the coating layers being scraped off and Zn deposition beneath the coating layer can unavoidably weaken the adhesion between the coating layer and the Zn substrate during the stripping/plating process.

Owing to its compatibility with existing battery technologies, the introduction of electrolyte additive is one of the most feasible procedures available today [23]. Various additives have been developed to date, including inorganic (MnSO4 [24], LiCl [25], monosodium glutamate [26], and Zn3(PO4)2 [27]), and organic (dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) [28], nicotinamide (NAM) [29], trimethyl phosphate (TMP) [30], polyethylene oxide (PEO) [31], and polyacrylamide (PAM) [32]) additives. Generally, electrolyte additives can assist in preventing dendrite formation in a variety of ways, including shielding the electrostatic field, increasing the number of Zn nucleation sites, tuning the Zn growth orientation, and regulating the solvation structure [23]. For instance, PAM can help enhancing the Zn anode reversibility by altering the nucleation sites and overpotential [32]. The Zn ions are transported to the surface via the acyl groups of the PAM molecules, which are adsorbed on a copper mesh substrate by the PAM molecules in the electrolyte. The electrostatic attraction of the PAM molecules to the Zn ions efficiently increases the number of nucleation sites and contributes to generating a uniform electric field. These beneficial features ensure that Zn grows uniformly and forms a homogeneous deposition surface. Despite their initial success, all these methods face significant technological challenges in terms of practical and scalable manufacturing. Therefore, the development of new electrolyte additives is still a critical task for extending the lifespan of Zn anodes, particularly at high current densities and areal capacities.

Herein, functionalized graphene quantum dots (F-GQDs) modified with multiple functional groups were introduced as additives into electrolytes to achieve highly reversible Zn plating/stripping. The extremely high electronegativity of the F-GQDs enables their preferential adsorption on the Zn surface. The F-GQDs-decorated Zn anode exhibits favorable properties, such as high hydrophilicity, low Zn nucleation energy, and uniformly distributed electrical field. Compared to conventional electrolyte additives, the peculiarity of the F-GQDs lies primarily in their quantum size (∼5 nm) and abundance of active sites, which induce homogeneous Zn deposition during repeated plating/stripping, inhibiting the growth of Zn dendrites. Benefiting from the assistance of the F-GQDs, symmetric Zn||Zn cells could operate for more than 1800 h at 0.5 mA cm−2 and 0.25 mA h cm−2. Even more impressively, the symmetric cell operated for greater than 450 h at a high current density (10 mA cm−2) and areal capacity (5 mAh cm−2), which demonstrates its ability to meet the demands of practical applications. Furthermore, a Zn||MnO2 full battery assembled with F-GQDs-decorated Zn anodes exhibited markedly improved rate and cycling performances.

The F-GQDs were fabricated via a bottom-up method (as illustrated in Fig. 1
a), in which acetaldehyde and thiourea were chosen as carbon and nitrogen/sulfur sources, respectively, due to their excellent reactivity. The growth of GQDs is based on a polycondensation mechanism [33,34]. In particular, citric acid molecules are self-assembled and dehydrolyzed into graphene nanosheets, resulting in the formation of GQD cores with a significant number of carboxyl and hydroxyl terminal groups. Furthermore, thiourea molecules react with the terminal groups of the GQDs to form additional functional groups containing nitrogen and sulfur. As a result, the synthesized F-GQDs consist of nanoscale graphene with a large number of oxygen-, nitrogen-, and sulfur-functional groups. It is worth noting that the abundant functional groups enable F-GQD powders to be well dispersed in water. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images in Fig. 1 b and S1 show that the average diameter of the F-GQDs is ∼ 5 nm. The atomic force microscopy (AFM) image in Fig. S2 shows that the thickness of the F-GQDs is 1–3 nm, which corresponds to 3–6 layers of graphene [35]. In the UV − vis absorption spectra of F-GQDs (Fig. 1 c), the absorption band at 340 nm originates from the n → π* transition of CC groups [36]. The two additional adsorption bands at 424 and 582 nm are attributed to conjugated CO and CS bonds, respectively. It should be noted that the introduction of ZnSO4 into the F-GQDs does not affect their absorption properties. The F-GQD solutions show a blue light response upon exposure to a 532 nm laser ( Fig. S3 ). The photoluminescence peak is red-shifted and its intensity is slightly reduced with increasing wavelength, indicating the excitation-dependent feature. This result stems from the absorption bands at 440–550 nm of sulfur − and nitrogen − containing groups, consistent with previous results ( Fig. S4 ) [34,37]. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy measurements were performed to identify the functional groups of the F-GQDs (Fig. 1 d). The broad stretching bands at 3374, 3251, and 3078 cm−1 are assigned to -OH, -NH, and -CH groups, respectively. The -CO bond of the COOH group is responsible for the peak at 1690 cm−1, whereas the two peaks at 1591 and 1381 cm−1 correspond to -CC and –CH3 groups, respectively; in addition, the peaks at 1182 and 1080 cm−1 are ascribed to -OH and -CN groups, respectively. In addition to these peaks, the sharp peak at 2061 cm−1 can be attributed to the -SCN group [38]. Furthermore, the XPS survey spectrum displays four peaks at 152, 284, 399, and 530 eV, corresponding to S 2p, C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s levels, respectively, with relative contents of 2.6 %, 51.4 %, 6.6 %, and 39.4 % (Fig. 1 e). The high-resolution C 1 s spectrum can be deconvoluted into CC/CC (283 eV), CN/CS/CO (284 eV), and CO (287 eV) signals (Fig. S5 a) [39]. The three peaks in the high-resolution N 1 s spectrum of the F-GQDs are attributed to pyridinic (396 eV), pyrrolic (398 eV), and graphitic (400 eV) nitrogen (Fig. 1 f), whereas the two peaks in the high-resolution S 2p spectrum are assigned to S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 states (Fig. 1 g). The 530.1 and 531.2 eV peaks in the high-resolution O 1 s spectrum (Fig. S5 b) are ascribed to the stretching vibration of CO and –OH groups, respectively. In comparison, the XPS survey spectrum of GQDs synthesized without thiourea presents only O 1 s and C 1 s signals ( Fig. S6 ). The above analyses demonstrate the coexistence of functional groups such as –OH, –COOH, –NH2, and -SCN on the F-GQDs, which is beneficial for the electrolyte permeability and Zn deposition.

The CE is a key parameter for evaluating the reversibility of Zn plating/stripping. Thus, we assembled Zn||Ti half-cells using Zn as anode and Ti as cathode, and measured the plating/stripping voltage curves of the cells at 5 mA cm−2 and 2.5 mAh cm−2. The half-cells without F-GQDs display a voltage hysteresis of 84 mV (Fig. 2 c), while the value measured for the cells with F-GQDs is as low as 76 mV (Fig. 2 d). Furthermore, the half-cells with F-GQDs exhibit an average CE of 99.5 % after 450 cycles at 5 mA cm−2 and 2.5 mAh cm−2 (Fig. 2 e). In contrast, the Zn||Ti half-cells without F-GQDs present an unsatisfactory CE after 50 cycles, as a result of the continuous generation of dead Zn and the occurrence of parasitic reactions. The enhanced reversibility of the Zn plating/stripping process indicates that the protective layer of F-GQDs can eliminate harmful Zn dendrites and side reactions, thus enabling the application of the Zn anode. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurements of Zn||Ti half-cells also revealed the superior kinetics of the Zn anode protected with F-GQDs ( Fig. S12 ) [40]. Starting plating/stripping voltages of −0.062/0.013 and −0.077/0.021 V were observed for the F-GQDs-decorated Zn anode and for the Zn anode without F-GQDs in the electrolyte, respectively. The decreased nucleation overpotential of F-GQDs-decorated Zn anode indicates a lower Zn nucleation barrier, further demonstrating that the F-GQDs facilitate the Zn nucleation.

As shown in Fig. 4
a, the abundant functional groups of the F-GQDs can promote their interaction with Zn. These interactions were investigated via density functional theory (DFT) calculations to identify the possible mechanism of nanoscale-regulated zinc nucleation. The DFT results indicate that the adsorption energies of the functional groups on the Zn (002) (i.e., −4.15 eV for –NH2, −3.86 eV for –OH, and −3.40 eV for –COOH) are higher than that of H2O (-1.29 eV) (Fig. 4 b). This suggests that the F-GQDs are preferentially adsorbed on the Zn surface. As a result, they will replace the original water molecules on the Zn surface, isolating them and inhibiting water decomposition and Zn corrosion. To verify the multifunctional features of the F-GQDs, Zn corrosion and HER tests were carried out to assess the thermodynamic stability of the Zn anode. Linear polarization measurements performed with the F-GQDs show a positive shift of the corrosion potential (Fig. 4 c). In particular, the Zn anode in the electrolyte with F-GQDs exhibits a larger corrosion voltage (-0.990 V) than that without F-GQDs (-0.993 V), revealing a low tendency to undergo corrosion side reactions as a result of the blocking effect of the F-GQDs. Moreover, the addition of the F-GQDs markedly limits water decomposition, as reflected by the negatively shifted voltage and lower current for the HER ( Fig. S16 ). This can be ascribed to the fact that the adsorbed F-GQDs on the Zn surface may serve as obstacles shielding Zn from corrosion and HER by the electrolyte, resulting in a highly stable Zn anode. The F-GQDs with abundant functional groups would endow the Zn substrate with high surface energy, leading to the facile growth of Zn nuclei. As shown in Fig. 4 d, the contact angle of F-GQDs-decorated Zn can be estimated to be as low as 27°. In contrast, the Zn anode without F-GQDs shows an increased contact angle of 85° because of the hydrophobic characteristics of Zn metal. The higher surface wettability is beneficial to electrolyte penetration and reduces the interfacial energy barrier to induce uniform Zn deposition [42]. The advantages of the F-GQDs in enhancing the Zn anode kinetics were further demonstrated through DFT simulations. The calculations of the interaction between a foreign Zn2+ ion and Zn indicate that the adsorption energy of Zn2+ on the F-GQDs-coated Zn (002) facet (i.e., −1.39, −2.11, and −0.92 eV for –NH2, –OH, and –COOH, respectively) exceeds that on the Zn anode without F-GQDs (-0.35 eV) ( Fig. S17 ). The high adsorption capacity of Zn2+ at the anode–electrolyte interface contributes to homogenizing the Zn2+ flux, enabling an ordered Zn deposition without dendrites.

The electric field distribution near the Zn anode surface plays a critical role in the Zn nucleation at the anode–electrolyte interface [43]. To investigate the role of the F-GQDs in modulating the electric field distribution, a theoretical simulation was conducted using the COMSOL package. The simulated distribution of the electric field near the Zn anode in the electrolyte without F-GQDs during the Zn deposition process is shown in Fig. 4 e. The electric field intensity near the Zn anode in the electrolyte without F-GQDs is uneven, due to the random distribution of Zn nuclei on the anode, especially when highly charged areas are present near the Zn nuclei. The electric field intensity gradient induces preferential deposition of Zn2+ ions on the Zn nuclei during the Zn plating process, resulting in further growth of Zn dendrites. In contrast, the anodes coated by the F-GQDs benefit from an optimized Zn nucleation at the nanoscale, contributing to a more uniform electric field distribution (Fig. 4 f). Thus, the particle aggregation seen on the Zn anode in the electrolyte without F-GQDs is successfully avoided, enabling a homogeneous Zn deposition. This shows that the F-GQDs can not only promote a uniformly distributed Zn nucleation, but also guide Zn growth without dendrite formation. These results imply that the F-GQDs are capable of regulating the uniform nucleation and growth of Zn.

In order to evaluate the applicability of the F-GQDs in a practical battery system, Zn||MnO2 full batteries with MnO2 as a typical cathode material were assembled to explore the electrochemical performance. A MnO2 sample with a one-dimensional nanofiber structure and without any impurities was prepared ( Fig. S18 ) using a method reported in the literature [24]. The CV plot of the full cells in Fig. 5 a displays distinct redox peaks corresponding to the reverse Zn2+ intercalation/deintercalation reactions [44]. The Zn||MnO2 battery prepared with the F-GQDs added in the electrolyte presents a smaller anodic/cathodic separation and a higher current density response than that without the F-GQDs, which reflects a higher capacity and enhanced kinetics for the Zn2+ insertion/extraction reactions in the MnO2 cathode. After the first cycle, the subsequent CV profiles exhibit almost identical shapes ( Fig. S19 ), revealing reversible electrochemical processes. To further elucidate the effect of the F-GQDs on the Zn2+ intercalation/deintercalation behaviors, typical galvanostatic charge–discharge (GCD) curves were obtained for the batteries cycled at 0.2 A/g (Fig. 5 b). Compared to the electrolyte without F-GQDs, the GCD profiles show that the F-GQDs achieve a lower charge plateau and a higher discharge plateau. The initial discharge capacity of the Zn||MnO2 battery using the F-GQDs-based electrolyte is 271.8 mAh/g, which is higher than that of the cell without F-GQDs (227.6 mAh/g), in line with the CV results. The rate capabilities of both cells at various current densities are shown in Fig. 5 c, and the corresponding voltage profiles are displayed in Fig. S20 . In the electrolyte with F-GQDs, the battery delivers specific capacities of 283.0, 282.8, 265.4, 237.5, 179.0, and 85.8 mAh/g at rates of 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, and 5.0 A/g, respectively. In comparison, the battery without F-GQDs exhibits lower specific capacities of 236.2, 227.6, 211.3, 185.8, 171.3, 131.6, and 65.1 mAh/g at the same rates. Similarly, when the rate returns to the low value of 0.1 A/g, the obtained specific capacity is 315.3 mAh/g, which is higher than that measured for the Zn anode in the electrolyte without F-GQDs (241.1 mAh/g). The improved initial capacity stems from the high reversibility of Zn with the addition of F-GQDs, as reflected by symmetrical cells and half cells (Fig. 3). However, for bare Zn, there appear to be severe side reaction and dendrite formation, resulting in poor Zn reversibility. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) analysis reveals that the F-GQDs-based batteries have a lower charge-transfer resistance, which accounts for their superior rate performance (Fig. 5 d) [45]. In light of these findings, it can be inferred that the F-GQDs can limit side reactions and dramatically promote Zn utilization. On the contrary, the batteries without F-GQDs deliver a poor rate and low capacity, attributed to their poor CE and the instability of Zn metal. In addition to the enhanced rate performance, the F-GQDs-based full batteries also exhibit remarkable cycling stability, ascribed to the suppression of dendrite growth and side reactions (Fig. 5 e). Note that the full battery was activated first at low current density (0.2 A/g), and then it was charged/discharged at high current density (1.0 A/g). Therefore, these batteries yield an excellent initial specific capacity of 254.6 mAh/g, which remains stable at 200.1 mAh/g after 500 cycles, with a capacity retention of 78.6 % at 1 A/g. In contrast, the capacity of the full battery without F-GQDs markedly drops to 29.8 mAh/g after 500 cycles (initial capacity: 212.0 mAh/g, corresponding retention rate: 14.1 %). These findings provide clear evidence that the F-GQDs fabricated by in situ electrochemically induction on the Zn anode are able to regulate Zn plating/stripping and inhibit interfacial side reactions, achieving superior electrochemical properties in Zn||MnO2 full batteries under practical conditions.

In summary, functionalized GQDs with multiple functional groups were added into electrolytes to regulate Zn plating/stripping at the nanoscale. During Zn deposition, the F-GQDs are preferentially deposited on the Zn surface. The newly generated interface, with extremely high electronegativity, exhibits a low energy barrier for Zn nucleation and achieves uniform Zn deposition; this prevents water molecules from directly contacting the zinc interface, reduces the proportion of water molecules in the electric double-layer structure, and inhibits side reactions at the anode–electrolyte interface. A symmetrical Zn cell based on the designed electrolyte system exhibits an ultralong cycle life of 1800 h under the test condition of 0.5 mA cm−2, and can operate for more than 450 h even at 10 mA cm−2. More impressively, a Zn||Ti half-cell tested in a ZnSO4 electrolyte with added F-GQDs delivers a CE as high as 99.6 %, highlighting the outstanding reversibility of the Zn plating/stripping process. The application potential of the F-GQDs additive was confirmed by the considerable cycling stability of a Zn|MnO2 full battery. This nanoscale-regulated Zn nucleation strategy paves a fresh pathway that can be extended to investigate dendrite-free batteries beyond Zn.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korean government (MIST) (Nos. 2022R1F1A1074441 and 2022R1F1A1074707).
